items.title: Use Nextflow file() and toUriString() for cloud storage compatibility
items.body:
~Changes xmlPath input from val() to path() so Nextflow stages the file locally before processing.~
Uses Nextflow's file() function instead of Java's File class, and replace toString() with toUriString() to read XML content. This fixes "No such file or directory" errors when running on cloud platforms (AWS S3, Azure, GCS) via Seqera Platform.  

Noted in review for PR #120 

## PR checklist

- [x] This comment contains a description of changes (with reason).
- [ ] If you've fixed a bug or added code that should be tested, add tests!
- [ ] If you've added a new tool - have you followed the pipeline conventions in the [contribution docs](https://github.com/nf-core/mcmicro/tree/master/.github/CONTRIBUTING.md)
- [ ] If necessary, also make a PR on the nf-core/mcmicro _branch_ on the [nf-core/test-datasets](https://github.com/nf-core/test-datasets) repository.
- [ ] Make sure your code lints (`nf-core pipelines lint`).
- [ ] Ensure the test suite passes (`nextflow run . -profile test,docker --outdir <OUTDIR>`).
- [ ] Check for unexpected warnings in debug mode (`nextflow run . -profile debug,test,docker --outdir <OUTDIR>`).
- [ ] Usage Documentation in `docs/usage.md` is updated.
- [ ] Output Documentation in `docs/output.md` is updated.
- [ ] `CHANGELOG.md` is updated.
- [ ] `README.md` is updated (including new tool citations and authors/contributors).

items.title: Fix test failure formatting in PR comments - preserve newlines for code comparisons
items.body:
Test failures in PR comments were unreadable due to newline removal and markdown escaping. Java code comparisons appeared as single-line escaped strings in table cells.

## Changes

**Parse from stacktrace text instead of XML attribute**
- XML attributes normalize whitespace per spec - newlines become spaces
- Text content of `<failure>` element preserves formatting
- Added `parse_source` field that prefers text content when available

**New `parse_assertion_error()` function**
- Extracts expected/actual from `expected: <...> but was: <...>` pattern
- Handles alternative `Expected: ... Actual: ...` format
- Returns `None` for non-parseable errors (NPE, etc.)

**Sectioned layout replaces table format**
- Each test gets numbered section with class.method
- Expected/Actual in separate Java code blocks
- Stack traces in collapsible `<details>`
- Removed `escape_markdown()` - code blocks handle it naturally

## Example Output

**Before:**
```
| Test | Message |
| `test_import_migration` | expected: \<import org.junit.jupiter.api.Assertions.\*;\> but was: \<import org.junit.Assert.\*;\> |
```

**After:**
```markdown
### 1. MigrationTest.test_import_migration

**Expected:**
```java
import org.junit.jupiter.api.Assertions.*;
```

**Actual:**
```java
import org.junit.Assert.*;
```
```

## Files Changed
- `.github/scripts/extract_test_failures.py` - Complete rewrite of formatting logic
- `.gitignore` - Add Python cache exclusions

<!-- START COPILOT ORIGINAL PROMPT -->



<details>

<summary>Original prompt</summary>

> ## Problem
> 
> Die Formatierung der Testergebnisse im PR-Kommentar ist schwer lesbar. Die `escape_markdown()` Funktion entfernt alle Newlines und quetscht die Fehlermeldungen in eine Tabellenzeile, was besonders bei Java-Code-Vergleichen (expected vs. actual) unlesbar wird.
> 
> ## Aktuelles Ergebnis (schlecht lesbar)
> 
> ```markdown
> | Test | Class | Message |
> |------|-------|---------|
> | `handles\_wildcard\_static\_imports` | `MigrationEdgeCasesTest` | expected: \<package test; import static org.junit.jupiter.api.Assertions.\*; import org.junit.jupiter.api.Test; public class MyTest { @Test public void testAssertions() { assertEquals(42, 42); assertTrue(true); assertFalse(false); } } \> but was: \<package test; import static org.junit.Assert.\*; ... |
> ```
> 
> ## Gewünschtes Ergebnis (gut lesbar)
> 
> ```markdown
> ## ❌ Failed Tests Details
> 
> <!-- test-failures-comment -->
> 
> **8 test(s) failed**
> 
> ---
> 
> ### 1. MigrationEdgeCasesTest.handles_wildcard_static_imports
> 
> **Expected:**
> ```java
> package test;
> import static org.junit.jupiter.api.Assertions.*;
> 
> import org.junit.jupiter.api.Test;
> 
> public class MyTest {
>     @Test
>     public void testAssertions() {
>         assertEquals(42, 42);
>     }
> }
> ```
> 
> **Actual:**
> ```java
> package test;
> import static org.junit.Assert.*;
> 
> import org.junit.jupiter.api.Test;
> 
> public class MyTest {
>     @Test
>     public void testAssertions() {
>         assertEquals(42, 42);
>     }
> }
> ```
> 
> <details>
> <summary>📋 Full Stack Trace</summary>
> 
> ```
> org.opentest4j.AssertionFailedError: 
> expected: <...> but was: <...>
>     at org.junit.jupiter.api.AssertionFailureBuilder.build(...)
>     ...
> ```
> 
> </details>
> 
> ---
> 
> ### 2. MigrationAssertionsTest.migrates_static_import_wildcard
> 
> ...
> ```
> 
> ## Änderungen an `.github/scripts/extract_test_failures.py`
> 
> ### 1. Neue Funktion zum Parsen von expected/actual
> 
> ```python
> def parse_assertion_error(message):
>     """Parse expected/actual from assertion error message."""
>     # Pattern: "expected: <...> but was: <...>"
>     import re
>     match = re.search(r'expected:\s*<(.*)>\s*but was:\s*<(.*)>', message, re.DOTALL)
>     if match:
>         return {
>             'expected': match.group(1).strip(),
>             'actual': match.group(2).strip()
>         }
>     return None
> ```
> 
> ### 2. Neue Formatierung (keine Tabelle, separate Abschnitte)
> 
> ```python
> def format_as_markdown(failures):
>     """Format failures as Markdown for PR comment."""
>     if not failures:
>         return "## ✅ All Tests Passed\n\n<!-- test-failures-comment -->\nNo test failures detected."
>     
>     lines = [
>         "## ❌ Failed Tests Details",
>         "",
>         "<!-- test-failures-comment -->",
>         "",
>         f"**{len(failures)} test(s) failed**",
>         ""
>     ]
>     
>     for i, f in enumerate(failures, 1):
>         short_class = f["class"].split(".")[-1]
>         
>         lines.extend([
>             "---",
>             "",
>             f"### {i}. {short_class}.{f['test']}",
>             ""
>         ])
>         
>         # Try to parse expected/actual from message
>         parsed = parse_assertion_error(f["message"])
>         if parsed:
>             lines.extend([
>                 "**Expected:**",
>                 "```java",
>                 parsed['expected'],
>                 "```",
>                 "",
>                 "**Actual:**",
>                 "```java",
>                 parsed['actual'],
>                 "```",
>                 ""
>             ])
>         else:
>             # Fallback: show message as-is
>             lines.extend([
>                 "**Message:**",
>                 "```",
>                 f["message"],
>                 "```",
>                 ""
>             ])
>         
>         # Stack trace in collapsible section
>         lines.extend([
>             "<details>",
>             "<summary>📋 Full Stack Trace</summary>",
>             "",
>             "```",
>             f["stacktrace"],
>             "```",
>             "",
>             "</details>",
>             ""
>         ])
>     
>     return "\n".join(lines)
> ```
> 
> ### 3. Weniger aggressives Escaping
> 
> Die `escape_markdown()` Funktion wird nur noch für spezielle Fälle verwendet, nicht für den gesamten Text. Newlines bleiben erhalten.
> 
> ## Akzeptanzkriterien
> 
> - [ ] Expected/Actual werden als separate Code-Blöcke mit Java-Syntax-Highlighting angezeigt
> - [ ] Newlines bleiben erhalten für bessere Lesbarkeit
> - [ ] Jeder fehlgeschlagene Test hat seinen eigenen Abschnitt
> - [ ] Stack Traces sind in aufklappbaren Details-Abschnitten
> - [ ] Die Formatierung ist sowohl im GitHub-UI als auch für Copilot gut lesbar


</details>



<!-- START COPILOT CODING AGENT SUFFIX -->

*This pull request was created from Copilot chat.*
>

<!-- START COPILOT CODING AGENT TIPS -->
---

💡 You can make Copilot smarter by setting up custom instructions, customizing its development environment and configuring Model Context Protocol (MCP) servers. Learn more [Copilot coding agent tips](https://gh.io/copilot-coding-agent-tips) in the docs.

items.title: feat: v0.3.0
items.body:
## v0.3.0 — Grimoire

This branch takes cantrip from "spec-generated code that passes conformance tests" to "framework that works with real LLMs." It adds the Grimoire appendix to the spec, rewrites all examples as teaching programs, and fixes every framework bug that surfaced when running against gpt-5-mini and claude-sonnet.

69 files changed, +5250 / −2177 across py/clj/ex.

---

## What changed and why

### 1. Spec: Appendix A — Grimoire (5d354e6, 26c5f1b)

Twelve patterns (A.1–A.12) showing what you build with the spec's vocabulary, from a raw LLM query through the familiar. Synthesized from six drafts across three models (Claude, Codex, Gemini). Informative, not normative — no new conformance rules beyond chapters 1–7.

The spec itself was tightened from 17,062 → 11,528 words (−32%), all 77 behavioral rules preserved. Redundant prose cut, sacred passages (entity emergence, lethal trifecta, ghost library) kept at full strength. Version bumped 0.2.0 → 0.3.0.

### 2. Teaching examples replace conformance theater (a9884bf → afbda85)

The old examples were conformance theater — opaque helpers hiding construction, toy arithmetic intents, FakeLLM-only. The new examples are teaching programs:

- **Visible construction**: every Cantrip/Circle/Identity built inline with named fields
- **Realistic intents**: SaaS metrics analysis, Q3 revenue drivers, churn risk scoring
- **Spec vocabulary**: comments reference rule IDs (CIRCLE-1, WARD-1, COMP-7)
- **Narrative output**: `println`/`print()` tells the story as the example runs
- **Non-happy paths**: circle rejection, error steering, truncation, ward enforcement
- **Dual mode**: FakeLLM for CI, real LLM for demos (env vars control which)

Python: 12 examples. Clojure: 13 (extra ACP pattern). Elixir: 12.

### 3. Framework fixes — making real LLMs work

Running examples against real LLMs exposed bugs in all three implementations. These are the substantive framework changes, not just example tweaks.

#### Python (d111473, b70d4fd, d16c60e)
- **InProcessPythonExecutor**: real Python via `exec()` with gate functions injected, replaces MiniCodeExecutor (regex toy) as the default code medium executor
- **done gate schema**: `_DONE_PARAMETERS` gives done an `answer` param so LLMs know what to pass
- **tool_choice="required"**: when `require_done_tool=True`, forces LLM to use tools
- **Child identity**: generic prompt instead of inheriting parent's delegation instructions (which reference gates unavailable at child depth)
- **Subtractive builtins ward**: start with full builtins, subtract dangerous ones — matches spec's ward model
- **call_gate exposed**: code medium namespace now includes `call_gate()` matching capability_text
- **system_prompt in call_entity**: parent can set child identity via request
- **context in call_entity**: parent can pass context data, prepended to child intent

#### Clojure (4bfad34, e1a169b, b1670dd, d01a8f4, 6d23bb7)
- **clojure.data.json**: replaces 170-line hand-rolled JSON parser. The old parser was a liability — any escape handling edge case silently corrupted LLM code output going into SCI
- **max_completion_tokens: 16384**: prevents OpenAI API from silently truncating tool call arguments. Uses `max_completion_tokens` (not `max_tokens`) for gpt-5-mini compatibility
- **done gate answer schema**: LLMs know to pass `{answer: "..."}` not bare strings
- **String key support in done-observation**: real LLM JSON has string keys, not keywords
- **tool-call-id propagation**: observations carry IDs through the full pipeline
- **tool_choice :required**: auto-set when require-done-tool is true
- **Code medium tool-call synthesis**: wraps raw content responses in `clojure` tool-call format for OpenAI message threading
- **Gate schemas always include "properties"**: OpenAI requires it even for empty schemas
- **FakeLLM skips tool_choice enforcement**: scripted tests shouldn't fail on tool_choice validation
- **Capability text for code medium**: SCI sandbox physics + host function docs injected into LLM context. Correctly documents fire-and-forget semantics (call-gate/submit-answer emit, call-agent blocks and returns)
- **SCI capability text improvements**: explicit Java interop prohibition, no defprotocol/defrecord/deftype, synchronous call-agent semantics
- **Generic child system prompt**: children get "You are a child entity" instead of inheriting the parent's coordinator prompt that tells them to delegate recursively
- **Strip delegation gates from children**: `derive-child-cantrip` removes `call_entity`/`call_entity_batch` gates, preventing runaway recursion (matches TS reference)
- **Cap child max-turns at 3**: bounds error cascade duration when children write invalid SCI code
- **context and system_prompt in call-agent**: parent can pass context and override child identity
- **LLM timeout bumped to 120s**: code medium with real LLMs needs more time
- **dotenv .env loading**: examples load API keys from `.env` file

#### Elixir (4bfad34, 94b2457, b1670dd, d01a8f4, 6d23bb7)
- **done gate answer schema**: matching Clojure fix
- **put_in on Cantrip struct**: `put_in` doesn't work on Elixir structs (no Access protocol). Changed to `%{struct | field: value}` syntax. This was silently breaking ALL `call_entity` calls with `system_prompt`
- **Generic child system prompt**: same fix as Clojure — children don't inherit coordinator identity
- **defmodule prohibition in capability_presentation**: prevents LLM from writing `defmodule` in code medium
- **context and system_prompt in call_entity**: matching Python/Clojure
- **LLM timeout bumped to 120s**: matching Clojure
- **Example 12 rewrite**: uses `call_entity.()` binding instead of manual `Cantrip.new/1` construction (LLM can't know the LLM config tuple)

### 4. Prompt synthesis parity (e1a169b)

Code medium now injects sandbox physics and host function documentation into the LLM context across all implementations, matching TS's `capabilityDocs()` pattern:

- **Python**: `CodeMedium.capability_text()` → runtime injects as first system message
- **Clojure**: `capability-text` + `tool-view` → `build-messages` injects in runtime
- **Elixir**: `capability_presentation` (already had this)

Key insight: Clojure code medium is fire-and-forget (`call-gate` emits, doesn't return inline). Python code medium is synchronous (`call_gate` executes and returns). `call-agent` is synchronous in both. Capability text must reflect these differences per language.

---

## How it was built

Wave 1: Write grimoire examples for all three languages using parallel Codex agents with dense prompts (SPEC, TS reference examples, API surface docs, red-green workflow). All three produced passing code on first try.

Wave 2: Review agents found structural problems — Elixir recreated conformance theater with new names, Python used private APIs, Clojure had FakeLLM-only examples. Fix agents resolved: Elixir full rewrite, Python/Clojure targeted fixes.

Wave 3: Real LLM verification exposed framework bugs. Each bug was fixed, verified, and the next example tested. Python converged fastest (organic implementation). Clojure needed the most framework work (SCI sandbox + hand-rolled JSON). Elixir had one critical bug (`put_in` on structs).

Wave 4: Deep debugging of Clojure example 12 (familiar). Used stderr tracing in the runtime loop to observe turn-by-turn behavior. Found: children inherited coordinator prompt → tried to delegate recursively. Children inherited code medium → gpt-5-mini writes invalid SCI Clojure (Math/round, malformed parens, duplicate case constants). Fixed with generic child identity, delegation gate stripping, max-turns cap, proper JSON parser, and max_completion_tokens.

---

## Test results

| Language | Tests | Status |
|----------|-------|--------|
| Python | 227 passed, 2 skipped | All green |
| Clojure | 110 tests, 261 assertions | All green |
| Elixir | 170 tests, 0 failures | All green |
| TypeScript | 615 pass, 55 skip | Unchanged (reference impl) |

### Real LLM verification
- **Python**: all 12 examples verified with real LLM
- **Clojure**: examples 01–11 verified; example 12 completes but slow (~5–10 min)
- **Elixir**: examples 01, 04, 12 verified with gpt-5-mini

---

## Known limitations

**Clojure example 12 (familiar) is slow with real LLM.** gpt-5-mini consistently writes Java interop (`Math/round`, `Math/exp`) in SCI sandbox code despite capability text prohibiting it. Children error-steer through all turns before truncating. The framework handles this correctly (error observation → retry → truncation → parent sees failure) — it's a model capability issue, not a framework bug. A stronger Clojure-aware model or conversation-medium children would resolve it.

**Ghost implementations caveat.** py/clj/ex are spec-generated, not battle-tested like TS. This branch significantly closes the gap but the TS implementation remains the reference for edge cases.

items.title: Migrate maintenance scripts to Python

This commit converts the core maintenance scripts from Bash/PowerShell to Python.
- generate-stackbrew-library.sh -> generate-stackbrew-library.py
- update.sh -> update.py
- Removed generate-stackbrew-library.ps1 and update.ps1
- Updated toolbox/Dockerfile to use python:3-alpine and include the requests library.
- Updated toolbox.sh and GitHub Actions workflows to invoke the new Python scripts.
- Implemented architecture set intersection logic in generate-stackbrew-library.py.

Updates `org.springframework:spring-framework-bom` from 6.2.15 to 6.2.16
Release notes

Sourced from org.springframework:spring-framework-bom's releases.

v6.2.16

:star: New Features

  • Improve performance of hashcode calculations for request mappings #36297
  • Improve performance of HandlerMethod bean lookup #36296
  • Improve performance of validation groups determination #36295
  • Improve performance of single pattern request mappings #36294
  • Optimize NamedParameterUtils#buildValueArray by lazily fetching SqlParameter #36232
  • Consistently close streams through try-with-resources in FileCopyUtils #36224
  • SqlBinaryValue and SqlCharacterValue should support InputStream content with undetermined length #36220
  • DataBufferUtils.write() with NettyDataBuffer on JDK 25 hangs indefinitely #36189
  • WebClient (Reactor) attributes on Netty channel do not clear after connection release #36163
  • Reintroduce WebLogicJtaTransactionManager in Spring Framework 6.2.x #36152
  • DisconnectedClientHelper should detect presence of RestClientException and WebClientException separately #36150
  • Add DataAccessException and MessagingException to the excluded outermost exceptions in DisconnectedClientHelper #36135
  • Improve user check in TransportHandlingSockJsService #36129

:lady_beetle: Bug Fixes

  • Avoid lock congestion in ConcurrentReferenceHashMap #36308
  • Resolved HttpEntity Controller argument does not reflect mutated HTTP headers #36301
  • AbstractMessageConverter does not support wildcards in supported MIME types #36286
  • Make LocalEntityManagerFactoryBean#setDataSource work on Hibernate as well as EclipseLink #36272
  • Deadlock might occur when calling System.exit on startup (against multiple shutdown hooks) #36268
  • Netty4HeadersAdapter.remove returns empty list instead of null for non-existing key #36227
  • EclipseLinkConnectionHandle can fail against transaction isolation race condition #36166
  • WiretapConnector leaks data buffers when response body not consumed #36051
  • UriComponentsBuilder loses the fragment when it consists of only a single character #36035
  • SimpleBeanInfoFactory fails to reliably resolve read/write methods in type hierarchies with unresolved generics #36026

:notebook_with_decorative_cover: Documentation

  • Fix links to JUnit User Guide #36218
  • Fix LocalContainerEntityManagerFactoryBean#setPersistenceUnitName javadoc #36206
  • Update documentation on trailing slash handling where type-level @GetMapping("/base") is combined with method level @GetMapping("/") #36200
  • Update documentation on the MediaType used for ProblemDetail #36193
  • Replace getErrors() with getBindingResult() in examples #36172
  • Upgrade Antora dependencies #36106
  • Fix typos and grammar #36023

:hammer: Dependency Upgrades

  • Bump fast-xml-parser from 4.5.2 to 5.3.4 in /framework-docs #36239
  • Upgrade to ASM 9.9.1 and Objenesis 3.5 #36244
  • Upgrade to JUnit 5.14.2 #36148
  • Upgrade to Micrometer 1.15.9 #36290
  • Upgrade to Reactor 2024.0.15 #36289

items.title: [infra] feat(cdp): implement GivexDriver with real #id selectors, URL constants, and full happy-path purchase flow

labels.name: approved-override
labels.description: vars.CHANGE_CLASS_APPROVED

items.body:
- [x] Re-read blueprint and current CDP driver/tests for interaction requirements
- [x] Search the repository for scroll, coordinate-click, hover, and ghost-cursor implementations
- [ ] Compare actual implementation against blueprint-required behaviors
- [ ] Verify current local test/CI state relevant to the focused review
- [ ] Reply with a focused readiness verdict on the interaction-gap review

<!-- START COPILOT ORIGINAL PROMPT -->



<details>

<summary>Original prompt</summary>

Update `modules/cdp/driver.py` on branch `copilot/add-givex-driver-implementation` in repo `delaila5888749dhah/lush-worker-selector-devlush` to replace ALL `[TODO: ...]` selector placeholders with real selectors, and implement the complete happy-path flow including new methods for: egift form, add-to-cart, review-checkout, cart, guest checkout, billing, card payment, and state detection.

## Full happy-path flow to implement

### Bước 0 — Navigate
- URL_BASE = `https://wwws-usa2.givex.com/cws4.0/lushusa/`
- Cookie banner: `#button--accept-cookies`
- Buy eGift link: `#cardForeground a[href*='Buy-E-gift-Cards']`

### Bước 1 — eGift Page (`https://wwws-usa2.givex.com/cws4.0/lushusa/e-gifts/`)
Fields to fill:
- **To (Recipient Email)**: `input#giftCardEmail` or `input[name="giftCardEmail"]`
- **Recipient Name**: `input#recipientName` or `input[name="recipientName"]`
- **Message/Greeting**: `textarea#giftMessage` or `textarea[name="giftMessage"]`
- **From (Sender Name)**: `input#senderName` or `input[name="senderName"]`
- **Amount**: `input.denomination-input` or `input[name="amount"]` (type the amount value)

Actions:
- Click **ADD TO CART**: `button.add-to-cart-button` or `button[data-action="add-to-cart"]`
- Wait for **REVIEW & CHECKOUT** button to appear: `a.review-checkout-button` or `a[href*="cart"]`
- Click **REVIEW & CHECKOUT**

### Bước 2 — Cart & Guest Checkout
- At cart page: click **BEGIN CHECKOUT**: `button.begin-checkout` or `a[href*="checkout"]`
- At checkout page: click **CHECKOUT AS GUEST**: `#guestHeading` (this is a clickable heading/button)
- **Guest Email** field: `#guestEmail` or `input[name="guestEmail"]`
- Click **CONTINUE**: `button#guestContinue` or `button[data-action="guest-continue"]`

### Bước 3 — Shipping / Billing Form
Fields:
- First Name: `input#firstName` or `input[name="firstName"]`
- Last Name: `input#lastName` or `input[name="lastName"]`
- Address: `input#address1` or `input[name="address1"]`
- City: `input#city` or `input[name="city"]`
- State: `select#state` or `select[name="state"]`
- ZIP: `input#zipCode` or `input[name="zipCode"]`
- Phone: `input#phone` or `input[name="phone"]`
- Email: `input#email` or `input[name="email"]` (in billing section)
- Click **CONTINUE** to proceed to payment: `button.billing-continue` or `button[data-action="billing-continue"]`

### Bước 4 — Payment / Card
Fields:
- Card Number: `input#creditCardNumber` or `input[name="creditCardNumber"]`
- Expiry Month: `select#expirationMonth` or `select[name="expirationMonth"]`
- Expiry Year: `select#expirationYear` or `select[name="expirationYear"]`
- CVV: `input#securityCode` or `input[name="securityCode"]`
- Submit: `button.complete-purchase` or `button[data-action="complete-purchase"]` (text: "COMPLETE PURCHASE")

### Bước 5 — Post-submit States
- **Success**: URL contains `/order-confirmation` OR `/confirmation` OR element `.order-confirmation` present
- **VBV/3DS iframe**: `iframe[src*="3dsecure"]` OR `iframe[src*="adyen"]` OR `iframe[id*="threeds"]`
- **VBV Cancel button** (inside iframe): `button[id*="cancel"]` OR `a[id*="cancel"]` OR `button:contains("Cancel")` OR `a:contains("Return to")`
- **Declined**: `.payment-error` OR `.error-message` OR `div[data-error]` OR element containing text "declined" OR "transaction failed"
- **UI Lock / spinner**: `.loading-overlay` OR `.spinner` OR `div[aria-busy="true"]`
- **Error popup close**: `button.modal-close` OR `button[aria-label="Close"]` OR `.modal button[type="button"]`
- **Neutral div** (for ui_lock focus-shift): `body`

## Updated selector constants

Replace the selector section at the top of `driver.py` (lines 20-62) with these REAL selectors:

```python
# ── Navigation ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
SEL_COOKIE_ACCEPT   = "#button--accept-cookies"
SEL_BUY_EGIFT_BTN   = "#cardForeground a[href*='Buy-E-gift-Cards']"

# ── eGift form (Bước 1) ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────
SEL_RECIPIENT_EMAIL = "input[name='giftCardEmail'], input#giftCardEmail"
SEL_RECIPIENT_NAME  = "input[name='recipientName'], input#recipientName"
SEL_GREETING_MSG    = "textarea[name='giftMessage'], textarea#giftMessage"
SEL_SENDER_NAME     = "input[name='senderName'], input#senderName"
SEL_AMOUNT_INPUT    = "input.denomination-input, input[name='amount']"
SEL_ADD_TO_CART     = "button.add-to-cart-button, button[data-action='add-to-cart'], input[value*='ADD TO CART']"
SEL_REVIEW_CHECKOUT = "a.review-checkout-button, a[href*='cart'], button[data-action='review-checkout']"

# ── Cart & Guest Checkout (Bước 2) ───────────────────────────────────────────
SEL_BEGIN_CHECKOUT      = "button.begin-checkout, a.begin-checkout, a[href*='checkout']"
SEL_GUEST_HEADING       = "#guestHeading"
SEL_GUEST_EMAIL         = "#guestEmail, input[name='guestEmail']"
SEL_GUEST_CONTINUE      = "button#guestContinue, button[data-action='guest-continue']"

# ── Billing / Shipping form (Bước 3) ─────────────────────────────────────────
SEL_BILLING_FIRS...

</details>

items.title: docs(agent-auth): add Node.js SDK tabs and fix SDK method references
items.body:
## Summary

- Added Node.js SDK tabs to all Agent Auth pages (`quickstart.mdx`, `connected-accounts.mdx`, `tools/authorize.mdx`, `tools/execute.mdx`) using real methods from the `agent-auth-apis` branch (`@scalekit-sdk/node@2.2.0-beta.1`)
- Replaced fictional `agentConnect.accounts.*` methods in `connected-accounts.mdx` with real SDK calls for both Python and Node.js
- Restored `agent-auth/connected-accounts` in the sidebar (was previously commented out)
- Fixed Python `execute_tool` parameter: `tool=` → `tool_name=`
- Fixed status monitoring sections to use read-only lookups (`get_connected_account` / `getConnectedAccountByIdentifier`) instead of mutating `get_or_create` calls
- Added beta version `<Aside>` notes on pages using Node.js Agent Auth methods
- Fixed `tools/authorize.mdx` Python variable: `connect.` → `actions.`
- Added missing `python-dotenv` and `requests` to pip install in quickstart; added `import requests` to the init block
- Removed unused `Card`/`CardGrid` imports from component import lines

## Why

The Agent Auth docs were using a mix of fictional SDK methods and outdated Node.js exclusions. The Node.js SDK methods (`connectedAccounts.*`, `tools.*`) are now available in the `agent-auth-apis` beta branch. This PR aligns all documentation with the actual SDK source for both Python (`feature/api-tokens`) and Node.js (`agent-auth-apis`).

## Test plan

- [x] `pnpm --filter scalekit-docs build` passes
- [x] Pre-push hook build passes
- [ ] Verify all Agent Auth pages render with Python + Node.js tabs
- [ ] Verify `agent-auth/connected-accounts` is visible in the sidebar
- [ ] Smoke-test Python snippets against `scalekit-sdk-python` `feature/api-tokens` branch
- [ ] Smoke-test Node.js snippets against `scalekit-sdk-node` `agent-auth-apis` branch

<!-- This is an auto-generated comment: release notes by coderabbit.ai -->
## Summary by CodeRabbit

* **Documentation**
  * Rewrote authentication and connected-accounts docs to use SDK-driven examples instead of cURL/raw API snippets.
  * Reorganized content into per-language tabbed demos (Python, Node.js) covering create/retrieve/authorize/manage flows and tool execution.
  * Updated Python SDK examples to reflect API usage changes and fixed an "Authorization" typo.
  * Added dependency guidance and a Node.js agent-auth beta note; simplified OAuth/token guidance to SDK-managed flows.
<!-- end of auto-generated comment: release notes by coderabbit.ai -->

items.title: Version 2.0
items.body:
PyCript v2.0 is a complete rewrite from Python to **Java** and the **Montoya API**.

**Scripts from v1.x will NOT work with v2.0.**

### What Changed?
- ✅ No more Jython dependency - Pure Java implementation
- ✅ Faster performance - Native Java execution
- ✅ Raw data format - Passes raw text/binary instead of byte arrays
- ✅ WebSocket support - Merged [PyCript-WebSocket](https://github.com/Anof-cyber/PyCript-WebSocket) directly into PyCript
- ✅ Better binary and non ASCII data handling
     - Fixed #20 #21 #24 #25

## Key Features

- **WebSocket Support** - Encrypt/decrypt WebSocket messages
- **Binary & Text Encryption** - Supports both text-based and binary data
- **Better Performance** - Native Java implementation
- **Improved Logging** - Better debugging and error messages

items.title: [pull] main from lance-format:main
items.body: See [Commits](/Stars1233/lance/pull/185/commits) and [Changes](/Stars1233/lance/pull/185/files) for more details. ----- Created by [ **pull[bot]**](https://github.com/wei/pull) (v2.0.0-alpha.4) _Can you help keep this open source service alive? **[💖 Please sponsor : )](https://prod.download/pull-pr-sponsor)**_ --- ## Summary by cubic Consolidated the LanceNamespace interface into lance core for Python and Java, introduced a unified connect() factory and plugin registry, and bumped the project to 1.0.0-beta.12. Also improved DynamoDB reliability and made compaction surface rewrite transactions. - **Refactors** - Added org.lance.namespace.LanceNamespace in Java; updated imports and tests; pom now uses lance-namespace-apache-client. - Introduced lance.namespace in Python with a LanceNamespace ABC, connect() factory, and plugin registry; re-exported request/response models from lance_namespace_urllib3_client; updated tests and docs to use connect("dir"/"rest", ...). - Updated LanceNamespaceStorageOptionsProvider to the new interface in both languages. - Bumped all crates/bindings to 1.0.0-beta.12. - **Bug Fixes** - DynamoDB: added standard retry (max 5 attempts) and warn-level SDK error logging to reduce transient failures. - Compaction: compact_files(materialize_deletions=False) now produces Rewrite transactions; Python bindings map Rewrite types under LanceOperation. Written for commit b21872585560cc4538a1f1977d5ac89f799b75c2. Summary will update automatically on new commits.

items.title: [GH-2769] Improve raster loader, writer, and viz docs
items.body:
## Did you read the Contributor Guide?

- Yes, I have read the [Contributor Rules](https://sedona.apache.org/latest/community/rule/) and [Contributor Development Guide](https://sedona.apache.org/latest/community/develop/)

## Is this PR related to a ticket?

- Yes, and the PR name follows the format `[GH-XXX] my subject`. Closes #2769

## What changes were proposed in this PR?

Restructure and improve the raster loader, writer, and visualization documentation:

- **SQL Overview page**: Added a "Raster function list" section so rasters are discoverable from the main SQL entry point
- **Raster tutorial**: Rewrote "Load data" to lead with the `raster` data source for GeoTiff (instead of the deprecated `binaryFile` approach). Moved `binaryFile` to a secondary section for non-GeoTiff formats (NetCDF, Arc Grid). Expanded "Save to permanent storage" with writer options table and step-by-step guide. Clarified that `tileWidth`/`tileHeight` are optional.
- **New individual function pages**: Created `RS_AsArcGrid`, `RS_AsGeoTiff`, `RS_AsPNG`, `RS_AsCOG`, and `RS_AsRaster` pages under `Raster-Output/`, consistent with the existing per-function doc pattern
- **Raster-Functions.md index**: Added all 5 new functions to the appropriate tables
- **Removed `Raster-loader.md` and `Raster-writer.md`**: All content absorbed into the tutorial and individual function pages
- **mkdocs.yml**: Removed the two deleted nav entries
- **Fixed cross-links**: Updated all references (including in the Jupyter notebook) to point to new locations

## How was this patch tested?

Ran `mkdocs serve` locally and verified:
- All pages render correctly with no new warnings
- All cross-links resolve
- Nav structure is clean

## Did this PR include necessary documentation updates?

- Yes, I have updated the documentation.

items.title: Rewrite backend from Java Spring Boot + PostgreSQL to Python FastAPI + MongoDB
items.body:
Complete backend rewrite from Java/Spring Boot/PostgreSQL to Python/FastAPI/MongoDB while maintaining API contract compatibility.

## Architecture Changes

- **Framework**: Spring Boot 3.2 → FastAPI 0.104
- **Database**: PostgreSQL (relational) → MongoDB 7.0 (document store)
- **Driver**: JPA/Hibernate → Motor (async MongoDB driver)
- **Runtime**: Synchronous Java → Native async/await Python

## Implementation

### Core Application
- `app/main.py`: FastAPI app with lifespan management for DB connections
- `app/config/database.py`: Motor async client with credential masking in logs
- CORS configured for ports 5173, 3000 (Vite/React dev servers)

### Data Models
- `app/models/todo.py`: Pydantic v2 models with custom ObjectId handler
- TodoCreate/TodoUpdate/TodoResponse schemas with validation (title 1-200 chars, description max 1000)
- Proper `__get_pydantic_core_schema__` implementation (v2 compatible, not deprecated `__get_validators__`)

### API Endpoints
All REST endpoints preserved at `/api/todos`:
- `GET /api/todos?completed={bool}` - List with optional filter
- `GET /api/todos/{id}` - Retrieve by ObjectId
- `GET /api/todos/search?title={query}` - Case-insensitive regex search
- `POST /api/todos` - Create with auto timestamps
- `PUT /api/todos/{id}` - Partial update with timestamp refresh
- `DELETE /api/todos/{id}` - Single delete
- `DELETE /api/todos` - Bulk delete (dev/test only, warning added)

### Deployment
- Dockerfile: Multi-stage Python 3.11 slim image
- docker-compose.yml: MongoDB 7.0 + backend services
- fly.toml: Fly.io deployment config with health checks
- start-backend.sh: Dev setup with venv management

## Code Reduction
- Java backend: ~1000+ LOC with boilerplate
- Python backend: 380 LOC total
- Auto-generated OpenAPI docs replace manual Swagger config

## Example Usage

```python
# Async route handler with MongoDB
@router.post("", response_model=TodoResponse, status_code=201)
async def create_todo(todo: TodoCreate):
    db = await get_database()
    now = datetime.utcnow()
    todo_dict = {**todo.model_dump(), "createdAt": now, "updatedAt": now}
    result = await db.todos.insert_one(todo_dict)
    return todo_helper(await db.todos.find_one({"_id": result.inserted_id}))
```

## Security
- Credentials masked in connection logs
- Input validation via Pydantic constraints
- Proper HTTP status codes (400/404/422)
- CodeQL scan: 0 vulnerabilities

<!-- START COPILOT CODING AGENT SUFFIX -->



<!-- START COPILOT ORIGINAL PROMPT -->



<details>

<summary>Original prompt</summary>

# 📝 TodoList Application

A full-stack TodoList application built with **Vite + React** (frontend), **Java Spring Boot** (backend), and **PostgreSQL** (database) for OOP and Web & Internet courses.

## 🚀 Features

- ✅ Create, Read, Update, and Delete todos (CRUD operations)
- ✅ Mark todos as complete/incomplete
- ✅ Edit todo title and description
- ✅ Filter todos (All, Active, Completed)
- ✅ Responsive and modern UI
- ✅ RESTful API architecture
- ✅ PostgreSQL database integration
- ✅ JPA/Hibernate for ORM

## 🏗️ Project Structure

```
todolist-app/
├── backend/                    # Spring Boot backend
│   ├── src/
│   │   └── main/
│   │       ├── java/com/todolist/backend/
│   │       │   ├── controller/    # REST controllers
│   │       │   ├── model/         # Entity models
│   │       │   ├── repository/    # JPA repositories
│   │       │   └── service/       # Business logic
│   │       └── resources/
│   │           └── application.properties
│   └── build.gradle           # Gradle dependencies
│
└── frontend/                  # Vite + React frontend
    ├── src/
    │   ├── components/        # React components
    │   ├── services/          # API services
    │   ├── App.jsx            # Main app component
    │   └── main.jsx           # Entry point
    └── package.json           # NPM dependencies
```

## 📋 Prerequisites

Before running this application, make sure you have the following installed:

- **Java 17** or higher
- **Gradle 8.5+** (or use the included Gradle wrapper)
- **Node.js 18+** and **npm**
- **PostgreSQL 14+** (or Docker to run PostgreSQL in a container)

## 🗄️ Database Setup

### Option 1: Using Docker (Recommended)

The easiest way to run PostgreSQL is using Docker:

```bash
docker-compose up -d
```

This will start PostgreSQL on port 5432 with the database `todolist_db` already created.

### Option 2: Manual PostgreSQL Installation

1. Install and start PostgreSQL server

2. Create the database:
   ```sql
   CREATE DATABASE todolist_db;
   CREATE USER todouser WITH PASSWORD 'password';
   GRANT ALL PRIVILEGES ON DATABASE todolist_db TO todouser;
   ```

3. Update database credentials in `backend/src/main/resources/application.properties`:
   ```properties
   spring.datasource.url=jdbc:postgresql://localhost:5432/todolist_db
   spring.datasource.username=todouser
   spring.datasource.password=your_password
   ```

## 🚀 Running the Application

### Quick Start

For the easiest setup experience, use the provided shell scripts:

1. **Start the database** (if using Docker):
   ```bash
   docker-compose up -d
   ```

2. **Start the backend** (in one terminal):
   ```bash
   ./start-backend.sh
   ```

3. **Start the frontend** (in another terminal):
   ```bash
   ./start-frontend.sh
   ```

4. Open your browser at `http://localhost:5173`

### Manual Setup

#### Backend (Spring Boot)

1. Navigate to the backend directory:
   ```bash
   cd backend
   ```

2. Build and run the application:
   ```bash
   ./gradlew bootRun
   ```
   
   Or build the JAR and run it:
   ```bash
   ./gradlew clean build
   java -jar build/libs/todolist-backend-1.0.0.jar
   ```

3. The backend server will start at `http://localhost:8080`

#### Frontend (React + Vite)

1. Navigate to the frontend directory:
   ```bash
   cd frontend
   ```

2. Install dependencies:
   ```bash
   npm install
   ```

3. Start the development server:
   ```bash
   npm run dev
   ```

4. Open your browser at `http://localhost:5173`

## 🚀 Deployment to Fly.io

This application is ready to be deployed to Fly.io using the provided Dockerfile.

### Prerequisites

1. Install the Fly CLI: https://fly.io/docs/hands-on/install-flyctl/
2. Sign up for a Fly.io account: https://fly.io/app/sign-up

### Deployment Steps

1. **Login to Fly.io:**
   ```bash
   flyctl auth login
   ```

2. **Create a PostgreSQL database on Fly.io:**
   ```bash
   flyctl postgres create --name todolist-db --region iad
   ```

3. **Launch the application:**
   ```bash
   flyctl launch
   ```
   
   This will use the `fly.toml` configuration file. When prompted:
   - Choose your app name (or use the default)
   - Select your region
   - Don't create a database (we already created one)

4. **Attach the database to your app:**
   ```bash
   flyctl postgres attach todolist-db
   ```
   
   This will automatically set the DATABASE_URL environment variable.

5. **Set additional environment variables (if needed):**
   ```bash
   flyctl secrets set JWT_SECRET=your-secret-key
   ```

6. **Deploy the application:**
   ```bash
   flyctl deploy
   ```

7. **Check the deployment status:**
   ```bash
   flyctl status
   flyctl logs
   ```

Your application should now be available at `https://your-app-name.fly.dev`

### Updating the Deployment

To deploy changes:

```bash
flyctl deploy
```

## 🐳 Docker Deployment

The application includes a Dockerfile for containerized deployment.

### Build the Docker image:

First, build the JAR file:

```bash
cd backend
./gradlew clean build...

</details>



<!-- START COPILOT CODING AGENT TIPS -->
---

items.title: docs: add comprehensive coverage instrumentation research
items.body:
## Summary

Comprehensive cross-language research on code coverage instrumentation to inform the starlark-go-x API design.

## Languages Covered (1212 lines)

| Language | Mechanism | Key Insight |
|----------|-----------|-------------|
| **Python** | `sys.settrace` / `sys.monitoring` | PEP 669 dramatically reduces overhead |
| **JavaScript** | V8 built-in counters | Zero-overhead best-effort mode |
| **TypeScript** | Source map remapping | Same as JS with source maps |
| **Java** | JaCoCo bytecode manipulation | <10% overhead, transparent |
| **Kotlin** | Kover (IntelliJ/JaCoCo) | Kotlin-optimized option |
| **Go** | Source rewriting | ~3% overhead, portable |
| **C++** | gcov/llvm-cov | Compiler instrumentation |

## Includes for Each Language

- How coverage is enabled (flags, env vars, APIs)
- Instrumentation mechanism (compile-time vs runtime)
- Key APIs and callback interfaces with code examples
- Links to official documentation
- Links to actual implementation source code

## Key Design Recommendations

1. **Runtime hooks** - Starlark is interpreted, so runtime tracing like Python is most appropriate
2. **Callback interface** - Similar to `sys.settrace` with `OnStatement`, `OnCall`, `OnBranch`
3. **Data API** - Like Go's `runtime/coverage` for write/flush/reset
4. **Coverage modes** - `set` (boolean), `count` (execution count), `atomic` (thread-safe)

## Test plan
- [x] Document created and formatted
- [x] All links verified during research

items.title: Analysis: Repository already meets all standardization requirements
items.body:
## Summary

Comprehensive analysis confirms the repository already achieves full standardization across all 5 languages (C, C++, Java, JavaScript, Python). No changes required.

## Verification Results

**File Structure**: All 14 required files present in each language directory
- variables, operators, control-flow, loops, functions, arrays, strings, collections, oop, file-io, error-handling, pointers-memory, functional, regex

**Code Style**: Consistent quick-reference format across all 70 files
- Identical section structure: Syntax → Explanation → Code Example → Complexity → Common Pitfall ⚠️ → Quick Tip ✨ → Language-Specific Notes
- Proper markdown with syntax-highlighted code blocks

**Cross-Language Consistency**: Verified with runnable examples
- Identical variable names (`userAge`, `maxScore`) 
- Matching expected outputs across languages
- Enables direct syntax comparison

## Example Consistency

All languages use identical patterns:

```python
# Python
user_age = 25
max_score = 100
print(f"Age: {user_age}")
```

```java
// Java
int userAge = 25;
int maxScore = 100;
System.out.println("Age: " + userAge);
```

Both produce: `Age: 25` / `Max Score: 100`

## Status

Repository already provides unified quick-reference format. Natural file length variations (operators: 300+ lines, error-handling: 40-50 lines) reflect appropriate topic complexity for quick-reference use case.

<!-- START COPILOT ORIGINAL PROMPT -->



<details>

<summary>Original prompt</summary>

> ## Objective
> Standardize the code style and structure across all programming languages (C, C++, Java, JavaScript) to match the existing Python directory pattern for consistent, easy revision.
> 
> ## Current State Analysis
> The **Python/** directory has a well-organized structure with these files:
> - arrays.md
> - collections.md
> - control-flow.md
> - error-handling.md
> - file-io.md
> - functional.md
> - functions.md
> - loops.md
> - oop.md
> - operators.md
> - pointers-memory.md
> - regex.md
> - strings.md
> - variables.md
> 
> ## Requirements
> 
> ### 1. File Structure Consistency
> Ensure **C/**, **CPP/**, **Java/**, and **JavaScript/** directories all have the same file structure:
> - variables.md
> - operators.md
> - control-flow.md
> - loops.md
> - functions.md
> - arrays.md
> - strings.md
> - collections.md
> - oop.md
> - file-io.md
> - error-handling.md
> - pointers-memory.md (for C/C++ - focus on pointers; for Java/Python/JS - focus on memory management)
> - functional.md
> - regex.md
> 
> ### 2. Code Style Pattern to Follow
> Match the **Python directory's style**:
> - **Concise, quick-reference format** (NOT tutorial-style)
> - Clear section headers with `##` or `###`
> - Code blocks with syntax highlighting
> - Minimal but essential explanations
> - Practical, runnable examples
> - Comments in code when necessary
> - Consistent formatting across all files
> 
> ### 3. Content Structure for Each File
> Each `.md` file should follow this pattern:
> ```markdown
> # [Topic] in [Language]
> 
> ## Basic Syntax
> [Concise code examples]
> 
> ## Common Operations
> [Quick reference examples]
> 
> ## Key Methods/Functions
> [Essential operations with examples]
> 
> ## Practical Examples
> [Real-world usage patterns]
> ```
> 
> ### 4. Language-Specific Considerations
> - **C/C++**: Include pointer syntax, memory management, standard library usage
> - **Java**: Include class structure, common Java idioms, Collections framework
> - **JavaScript**: Include ES6+ syntax, async/await, array methods, DOM basics if relevant
> 
> ### 5. Cross-Language Consistency
> - Use similar examples across languages where possible (e.g., same array operations)
> - This helps developers quickly compare and switch between languages
> - Maintain the "quick revision" purpose - easy to scan and recall
> 
> ## Success Criteria
> - [ ] All 5 language directories (C, CPP, Java, JavaScript, Python) have identical file structure
> - [ ] Code formatting is consistent across all languages
> - [ ] Each language has complete, practical examples for each topic
> - [ ] Style matches Python directory: concise, scannable, quick-reference format
> - [ ] All code examples are correct and runnable
> - [ ] Markdown formatting is clean and consistent
> 
> ## Implementation Notes
> - Preserve any good existing content from C, CPP, Java, JavaScript directories
> - Enhance or rewrite content to match the Python pattern where needed
> - Ensure code examples are practical and demonstrate best practices for each language
> - Keep explanations brief but clear - this is a quick-ref, not a tutorial
> 
> ## Expected Outcome
> A unified quick-reference repository where developers can:
> 1. Quickly revise any language with the same mental model
> 2. Easily compare syntax across languages
> 3. Find what they need fast without lengthy explanations
> 4. Use consistent navigation across all languages


</details>

items.body:
## Description

Replaces the Python-based webhook-ingest service with a modern TypeScript implementation using Hono and NATS JetStream. Also improves scripts quality and consistency.

### Key Changes

**webhook-ingest (Python → TypeScript)**
- Complete rewrite in TypeScript using Hono framework
- GitHub/GitLab webhook signature verification (HMAC-SHA256)
- NATS JetStream publishing with retry logic and deduplication
- Bounded publish retries (9s timeout) to stay within provider limits
- Proper health checks with NATS connectivity verification
- 21 unit tests

**Scripts Improvements**
- Convert postprocess-openapi-java.mjs to TypeScript
- Add explicit node:process imports (Node.js best practice)
- Fix biome schema version consistency (2.3.8)
- Remove duplicate erd:generate script
- Make DATABASE_URL optional for OpenAPI export
- Add @types/pg and @nats-io/* as explicit dependencies

## How to Test

CI covers this. Also:
- `npm run check:webhook-ingest` - format/lint/typecheck
- `npm run test:webhook-ingest` - 21 unit tests
- `npm run typecheck:scripts` - script validation

<!-- This is an auto-generated comment: release notes by coderabbit.ai -->
## Summary by CodeRabbit

* **New Features**
  * New TypeScript/Hono webhook ingest service for GitHub & GitLab with health/status endpoints, NATS JetStream publishing, and CLI utilities for extracting webhook examples and ERD generation.

* **Improvements**
  * Migration of intelligence and webhook services to TypeScript; unified npm-centric CI/build/lint/test flows; improved signature/token verification, deterministic deduplication IDs, and containerized runtime.

* **Tests**
  * Added unit tests for crypto verification, dedupe ID generation, routes, and subject parsing.

* **Documentation**
  * Docs updated to reflect TypeScript toolchain, setup, and deployment guidance.

items.title: Fix safe-inputs `script:` runner always returning `{"stdout":"","stderr":""}`
items.body:
- [x] Understand the bug: `generateSafeInputJavaScriptToolScript` generates a `.cjs` that only exports `execute()` but never calls it when run as subprocess
- [x] Fix `generateSafeInputJavaScriptToolScript` in `pkg/workflow/safe_inputs_generator.go` to add a `require.main === module` block that reads stdin, calls `execute()`, and writes JSON to stdout
- [x] Refactor main execution block to use async/await instead of Promise chaining
- [x] Move the main execution harness to `actions/setup/js/safe-inputs-runner.cjs`; generated scripts delegate via `require('./safe-inputs-runner.cjs')(execute)`
- [x] Add `actions/setup/js/safe-inputs-runner.test.cjs` with 4 Vitest tests (happy path, empty stdin, JSON parse error, execute error)
- [x] Update `actions/setup/setup.sh` to copy `safe-inputs-runner.cjs` to the safe-inputs directory
- [x] Update test in `safe_inputs_generator_test.go` to assert `require('./safe-inputs-runner.cjs')` delegation
- [x] Fix TS18046: `catch (e)` variable is `unknown`; use `e instanceof Error ? e.message : String(e)` guard
- [x] All tests pass: `make fmt`, TypeScript check, Vitest tests

<!-- START COPILOT ORIGINAL PROMPT -->



<details>

<summary>Original prompt</summary>

> 
> ----
> 
> *This section details on the original issue you should resolve*
> 
> <issue_title>Bug: `safe-inputs` with `script:` always returns `{"stdout":"","stderr":""}`</issue_title>
> <issue_description># Bug: `safe-inputs` with `script:` always returns `{"stdout":"","stderr":""}`
> 
> ## Summary
> 
> A `safe-inputs` entry using the `script:` runner always produces `{"stdout":"","stderr":""}` when called, regardless of what the script does. The return value is never surfaced to the agent.
> 
> ## Steps to Reproduce
> 
> Define a `safe-input` with `script:`:
> 
> ```yaml
> safe-inputs:
>   github-graphql:
>     inputs:
>       query:
>         type: string
>         required: true
>       variables:
>         type: string
>         required: false
>     env:
>       GITHUB_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}
>     script: |
>       const graphql = require('@octokit/graphql').graphql.defaults({
>         headers: { authorization: `token ${process.env.GITHUB_TOKEN}` }
>       });
>       const vars = inputs.variables ? JSON.parse(inputs.variables) : {};
>       const result = await graphql(inputs.query, vars);
>       return result;
> ```
> 
> Call it from a workflow. The agent receives:
> 
> ```
> {"stdout":"","stderr":""}
> ```
> 
> ## What I Tried
> 
> - Using `return result` (object)
> - Using `console.log(JSON.stringify(result))` — no output in stdout
> - Simplified scripts (e.g. `return { viewer: { login: "test" } }`) — still empty
> - The same logic works fine with `py:` using `print(json.dumps(...))` — the Python runner correctly captures stdout
> 
> ## Expected Behavior
> 
> The `script:` runner should:
> 1. Capture the `return` value and serialize it as the tool output as documented
> 
> ## Actual Behavior
> 
> Every `script:` safe-input returns `{"stdout":"","stderr":""}` unconditionally. The agent sees no data and has to assume defaults / skip logic that depended on the result.
> 
> ## Impact
> 
> Workflows that rely on `script:` for GitHub GraphQL queries (or any JS-based safe-input) silently fail. The only workaround is to rewrite the logic in Python using `py:` or use `run:` with shell commands, which is significantly less ergonomic for GraphQL/JSON workloads.
> 
> ## Environment
> 
> - `gh aw` (github/gh-aw) — current version in use
> - Runner: GitHub Actions (AWF sandbox)
> </issue_description>
> 
> ## Comments on the Issue (you are @copilot in this section)
> 
> <comments>
> </comments>
> 


</details>

items.body: This PR refactors several functions and blocks to resolve naming conflicts where local variables unintentionally shadowed outer-scope identifiers. By renaming these variables, we improve code clarity, prevent accidental reference issues, and adhere to best practices for maintainable code. - Re-defined variable from outer scope: DeepSource flagged instances where inner variables (e.g., `app`, `origin`, `allowed_origins`) reused names from outer scopes, risking unintended behavior and confusion. We renamed `app` to `fastapi_app`, `origin` to `origin_url`, `allowed_origins` to `env_allowed_origins`, and updated the Gradio block name from `demo` to `demo_interface`, along with corresponding docstring and call-site adjustments to ensure each identifier is unique and descriptive. > This Autofix was generated by AI. Please review the change before merging. --- ## Summary by cubic Refactored variable names to eliminate shadowing in the FastAPI lifespan manager, CORS origin validation, and Gradio interface. This prevents accidental outer-scope references and improves readability. - **Refactors** - lifespan(app) -> lifespan(_fastapi_app) with matching docstring update. - validate_origin(origin) -> validate_origin(origin_url); allowed_origins -> env_allowed_origins; updated regex checks to use new names. - Removed Gradio Blocks alias "demo" to avoid shadowing. Written for commit ec730aa285d6a735edb666ba9137bb07789596c0. Summary will update on new commits. Review in cubic ## Summary by Sourcery Refactor variable names to eliminate shadowing and improve clarity in API lifespan management, CORS origin validation, and the Gradio interface setup. Enhancements: - Rename FastAPI lifespan function parameter to avoid shadowing the application instance name. - Rename CORS origin validation parameters and environment-derived origins list to prevent variable shadowing and clarify their roles. - Rename the Gradio Blocks context variable in the UI setup to avoid name conflicts and better describe its purpose.

items.title: fix(python): emit Pydantic field aliases to fix nested field validation
items.body:
## Description
Linear ticket: [FER-8077](https://linear.app/buildwithfern/issue/FER-8077/elevenlabs-pydantic-v2-decoding-error-for-nested-field) 
<!-- Use "Closes" to automatically close the ticket when PR merges, or "Refs" to link without closing -->
<!-- Provide a clear and concise description of the changes made in this PR -->

A previous fix, #11011 only helped when callers invoked `model_validate()` directly, but it did not apply to nested validation paths (e.g. when a parent model is validated via `TypeAdapter`), so wire keys like `is_base64_encoded` could still fail with “Field required” inside nested objects. With this PR we will now emit real `pydantic.Field(alias=...)` for aliased fields and update core parsing utilities so nested/union validation works reliably across Pydantic v1 and v2. The fix preserves Python attribute names and adds safe handling for Python-field-name inputs with collision protection.

## Changes Made
<!-- List the main changes and updates implemented in this PR -->
- Aliased fields are now generated as `pydantic.Field(alias="<wire_name>")`, ensuring Pydantic validates wire keys correctly (including nested `TypeAdapter` cases).
- `parse_obj_as` now conditionally pre-dealiases only when needed (TypedDict / FieldMetadata-only paths), avoiding regressions with models that have native Pydantic aliases.
- Added a pre-validation rewrite from python field names to aliases with explicit collision detection
- Removed obsolete override: dropped the Pydantic v2 `model_validate` override in `UncheckedBaseModel` since aliasing is handled natively.
 
## Testing
<!-- Describe how you tested these changes -->
- [x] Unit tests added/updated
- [x] Manual testing completed


<!-- CURSOR_SUMMARY -->
---

> [!NOTE]
> Ensures wire-key validation works reliably (including nested `TypeAdapter`/unions) by moving to native Pydantic aliases and tightening core parsing.
> 
> - Generate aliased fields with `pydantic.Field(alias="...")`; update codegen to always set alias when a field is aliased
> - Update `parse_obj_as` to only pre-dealias when required (e.g., TypedDict or FieldMetadata-only), preserving native alias handling
> - Add pre-validation rewrite from Python field names to aliases with explicit collision detection in `UniversalBaseModel` (v1/v2)
> - Remove the Pydantic v2 `model_validate` override from `UncheckedBaseModel`
> - Add hardened v1-on-v2 core utilities (checks against `pydantic.v1.BaseModel`) and a regression test
> - Regenerate seed SDKs to reflect new aliasing and utilities; incidental lockfile bumps
> 
> <sup>Written by [Cursor Bugbot](https://cursor.com/dashboard?tab=bugbot) for commit 0a75f340cb488a692a3db75a53106b3c8739ca9b. This will update automatically on new commits. Configure [here](https://cursor.com/dashboard?tab=bugbot).</sup>
<!-- /CURSOR_SUMMARY -->

items.title: Extract LXST-kt as standalone library with full audio pipeline
items.body:
## Summary

- **Extracts the `:lxst` module into a standalone library** ([LXST-kt](https://github.com/torlando-tech/LXST-kt)) consumed via git submodule + Gradle composite build
- **Builds the complete Kotlin audio pipeline** (Phases 7–11): codecs, sources/sinks, mixer, network bridge, telephony state machine
- **Removes dead Python LXST dependencies** (numpy, pycodec2, chaquopy_audio_backend) — Python is only used for Reticulum/LXMF transport now
- **Makes LXST-kt transport-agnostic** — ready to swap Python Reticulum for a future Kotlin Reticulum with no refactoring

## What's included

### Kotlin Audio Pipeline (Phases 7–11)
- **Phase 7 — Codec Foundation**: Opus (9 profiles) and Codec2 (7 modes) with JNI, MIT-licensed wrappers
- **Phase 8 — Audio Sources & Sinks**: LineSource (mic), LineSink (speaker), float32/int16 conversion
- **Phase 9 — Mixer & Pipeline**: Multi-source mixer, ToneSource (382Hz dial tone), Pipeline orchestration
- **Phase 10 — Network Bridge**: PacketRouter (outbound/inbound packets + signalling), AudioPacketHandler interface
- **Phase 11 — Telephony**: Telephone state machine (897 lines), NetworkTransport interface, PythonNetworkTransport, Profile sealed class, mute/speaker/audio feedback

### LXST-kt Extraction
- Standalone Android library at `LXST-kt/` (git submodule)
- `bridge/` package renamed to `core/` with class renames:
  - `CallBridge` → `CallCoordinator`
  - `NetworkPacketBridge` → `PacketRouter`
  - `KotlinAudioBridge` → `AudioDevice`
  - `KotlinAudioFilters` → `AudioFilters`
  - `PythonCallManagerInterface` → `CallController`
- Columba consumes via `includeBuild("LXST-kt")` with dependency substitution

### Transport-Agnostic Renames
- `onPythonPacketReceived()` → `onInboundPacket()`
- `onPythonSignalReceived()` → `onInboundSignal()`
- `pythonCallManager` → `callController`
- All Python/Chaquopy-specific naming removed from LXST-kt interfaces

### Dead Code Removal
- Removed `chaquopy_audio_backend.py` (617 lines) — unused since Kotlin pipeline
- Removed `numpy` pip dependency — only used by dead audio backend
- Removed `pycodec2` pip dependency and wheels — codecs are now native JNI
- Removed `set_audio_bridge()` plumbing from PythonWrapperManager and reticulum_wrapper

### Bug Fixes
- AIDL `oneway` audit to prevent ANR on fire-and-forget IPC methods
- Speaker toggle via AudioTrack recreation
- Jitter-absorbing playback with time-based buffering
- Codec2 bit-packing fix (was destroying 7/8 of encoded data)
- Incoming call answer button fix
- Replaced wuqi-opus with libopus-android for native 48kHz decode
- GPL-2.0 codec2_talkie JNI wrappers replaced with MIT-licensed equivalents

## Architecture

```
Telephone (state machine, mute, audio feedback)
    │
    ▼
NetworkTransport (interface) ← swap-point for Kotlin Reticulum
    │
    ▼
PythonNetworkTransport (wraps call_manager.py via Chaquopy)
    │
    ▼
PacketRouter + call_manager.py → RNS (Python Reticulum)
```

## Test plan

- [x] 101+ unit tests pass (ProfileTest: 42, TelephoneTest: 59+)
- [x] LXST-kt builds standalone (`./gradlew :lxst:compileDebugKotlin`)
- [x] Columba builds with composite build (`./gradlew :app:compileDebugKotlin`)
- [x] Bidirectional voice calls tested on two physical devices
- [x] Speaker toggle, mute, call teardown verified on device

🤖 Generated with [claude-flow](https://github.com/ruvnet/claude-flow)

items.title: Switch from pyenv to deadsnakes PPA for Python management
items.body:
Resolves #326

## Summary
Replace pyenv-based Python installation with system-level packages from deadsnakes PPA, using update-alternatives for version switching.

## Changes

### packer/base/install/install_python.sh
- Complete rewrite to use deadsnakes PPA instead of pyenv
- Installs Python 2.7 and 3.10 via apt packages
- Configures update-alternatives with proper priorities (2.7=1, 3.10=2)
- Includes Python 2.7 pip support for Cassandra 3.x compatibility
- Removes .bash_profile modifications (no longer needed)

### packer/cassandra/bin/use-cassandra  
- Replace pyenv global command with update-alternatives --set
- Adds validation to ensure requested version is installed
- Extracts major.minor version from full version string
- Consistent with existing Java version management pattern

### packer/base/base.pkr.hcl
- Update comment to reflect new approach

## Benefits
- Faster installation (no compilation required)
- System-integrated packages with automatic security updates
- Consistent with existing Java version management using update-alternatives
- Reduced dependencies (no build tools needed just for Python)
- Better caching through apt package mirrors

## Testing
- All packer Docker tests pass (testPackerBase, testPackerCassandra)
- Python 2.7 and 3.10 installation verified
- Version switching logic validated
- iostat-tool installation working correctly

## Notes
- This changes the Python installation method in AMIs
- Existing pyenv-based AMIs will not be compatible
- New AMIs will use deadsnakes PPA packages
- Python 2.7 pip included for Cassandra 3.x compatibility

items.title: Release 1.12.2
items.body:
This pull request introduces a new privacy-preserving record linkage (PPRL) demo, improves CI support for multiple Spark versions, and enhances dependency management for Python projects. The most significant changes are the addition of an end-to-end demo for OpenToken, expansion of CI test coverage to Spark 3.4, 3.5, and 4.0, and improved automation for dependency updates.

**Demo and Documentation Enhancements:**

* Added a "PPRL Superhero Demo" section to the `README.md`, including a description and instructions for a new beginner-friendly, end-to-end example. [[1]](diffhunk://#diff-b335630551682c19a781afebcf4d07bf978fb1f8ac04c6bf87428ed5106870f5R9) [[2]](diffhunk://#diff-b335630551682c19a781afebcf4d07bf978fb1f8ac04c6bf87428ed5106870f5R24-R36)
* Added a new `run_end_to_end.sh` script and an `analyze_overlap.py` analysis script to the `demos/pprl-superhero-example/` directory, allowing users to generate datasets, tokenize them, and analyze overlap between records in a privacy-preserving way. [[1]](diffhunk://#diff-a69218e77913d83d23cd231811e89ff6532b3d979976536fb3fb2646ae6d8652R1-R81) [[2]](diffhunk://#diff-b24c0aea8f2ab0e38c15ded6dd9754b0ee37b1594091c1f8c88c1f52f3df316bR1-R294)

**CI/CD and Testing Improvements:**

* Updated `.github/workflows/ci.yml` to test the PySpark bridge against Spark versions 3.4, 3.5, and 4.0, including dynamic installation of the appropriate dependencies, and improved artifact naming for test results and coverage reports. [[1]](diffhunk://#diff-b803fcb7f17ed9235f1e5cb1fcd2f5d3b2838429d4368ae4c57ce4436577f03fR244) [[2]](diffhunk://#diff-b803fcb7f17ed9235f1e5cb1fcd2f5d3b2838429d4368ae4c57ce4436577f03fR254-L265) [[3]](diffhunk://#diff-b803fcb7f17ed9235f1e5cb1fcd2f5d3b2838429d4368ae4c57ce4436577f03fL274-R311) [[4]](diffhunk://#diff-b803fcb7f17ed9235f1e5cb1fcd2f5d3b2838429d4368ae4c57ce4436577f03fL329-R341)

**Dependency Management:**

* Enhanced `.github/dependabot.yml` to manage dependencies for both `opentoken-cli` and `opentoken-pyspark` Python packages, and broadened the Maven update scope to the entire `lib/java` directory. [[1]](diffhunk://#diff-dd4fbda47e51f1e35defb9275a9cd9c212ecde0b870cba89ddaaae65c5f3cd28L9-R9) [[2]](diffhunk://#diff-dd4fbda47e51f1e35defb9275a9cd9c212ecde0b870cba89ddaaae65c5f3cd28R37-R56)

**Developer Experience:**

* Updated `.vscode/settings.json` to set a default Markdown formatter for a better editing experience.

**Build Configuration:**

* Fixed a minor formatting issue in `.bumpversion.cfg` for the PySpark setup configuration.

items.title: Rewrite all examples using standardization
items.body:
### Description

This PR rewrites all examples in C/C++, C#, and Python to use standardized approaches for running inference with ONNX Runtime GenAI. It also removes the SLM engine, chat app, and Genny examples since they are now deprecated and not maintained. Users can try out [Foundry Local](https://foundrylocal.ai), which is powered by ONNX Runtime GenAI, if interested in similar examples.

### Motivation and Context

Examples across the languages and across functionalities are often out-of-sync. For example, a Python example may get updated but its equivalent C/C++ and C# examples do not. This PR fixes this by synchronizing feature parity across the various examples.

We can use this standardization to continue maintaining feature parity in the future.

items.body: ### Describe your changes: Fixes I worked on ... because ... # ### Type of change: - [ ] Bug fix - [ ] Improvement - [ ] New feature - [ ] Breaking change (fix or feature that would cause existing functionality to not work as expected) - [ ] Documentation # ### Checklist: - [x] I have read the [**CONTRIBUTING**](https://docs.open-metadata.org/developers/contribute) document. - [ ] My PR title is `Fixes : ` - [ ] I have commented on my code, particularly in hard-to-understand areas. - [ ] For JSON Schema changes: I updated the migration scripts or explained why it is not needed. ---- ## Summary by Gitar - **Caching optimization:** - Added `_chain_metrics` cache to `BurstIQClient.get_chain_metrics()` to avoid repeated API calls - **Data model fixes:** - Updated `TQLRecord.to_record()` to handle non-dict `data` values and merge with extra fields - **Performance tuning:** - Increased `_PAGE_SIZE` from 1,000 to 5,000 in BurstIQ sampler - **Test updates:** - Refactored pagination test to use `_PAGE_SIZE` constant for maintainability - **Documentation:** - Added `BurstIQ.md` with connection requirements and configuration details This will update automatically on new commits.

items.title: refactor(ci): Enhance cicddiagnostics skill Investigation flexib (#33760)
items.body:
## Description

Refactored cicd-diagnostics skill from bash to Python for improved compatibility, maintainability, and Claude Code skill standards compliance.

## Motivation for Python Migration

The original bash implementation had several critical issues that necessitated the migration to Python:

### 1. Non-standard for Claude Code Skills
- Python is the **recommended and standard language** for Claude Code skills
- Better integration with Claude Code's error handling and output formatting
- Structured output and parameter parsing that Claude can reliably interpret

### 2. Shell Compatibility Problems
- **Bash-specific features** don't work across all shell environments (bash vs zsh vs sh)
- Array handling differs between shells, causing failures on different systems
- String manipulation and advanced features have varying support
- Portability issues across macOS (zsh default), Linux (bash), and CI environments

### 3. Limited Error Handling
- Bash error handling is primitive (set -e, exit codes)
- Difficult to provide meaningful error messages to Claude
- Hard to implement graceful failure recovery
- Debugging bash scripts is significantly more difficult

### 4. Parameter Validation Complexity
- Validating complex parameters in bash is verbose and error-prone
- Type checking is difficult (everything is a string)
- Parsing structured data (JSON, arrays) requires external tools (jq, etc.)

### 5. External Service Detection Requirements
- Need robust HTTP libraries for GitHub API status checks
- JSON parsing for dependency health checks
- Better error handling for network failures

## Benefits of Python Implementation

- **Standard library richness**: Built-in JSON, HTTP, regex, data structures
- **Superior error handling**: Try/except with meaningful exceptions
- **Type hints**: Clear parameter types and validation
- **Maintainability**: More readable, easier to extend
- **Cross-platform**: Consistent behavior across environments
- **Testing**: Better unit testing capabilities

## Changes

- Converted all bash diagnostic scripts to Python modules
- Added parameter validation with clear error messages
- Implemented external issue detection (GitHub API status, dependency checks)
- Enhanced investigation workflow with decision-tree approach
- Improved documentation with Python examples
- Added comprehensive error handling and logging

## Testing

- Tested investigation workflows with various failure types
- Validated parameter parsing and error handling
- Verified external issue detection capabilities
- Confirmed compatibility across different environments

Closes #33760

This PR fixes: #33760

- Clean up the error message when a recipe cannot be found in the marketplace by [@​shanman190](https://redirect.github.com/shanman190) in [#​6688](https://redirect.github.com/openrewrite/rewrite/pull/6688)

**Full Changelog**: <https://github.com/openrewrite/rewrite/compare/v8.72.7...v8.72.8>

### [`v8.72.7`](https://redirect.github.com/openrewrite/rewrite/releases/tag/v8.72.7): 8.72.7

[Compare Source](https://redirect.github.com/openrewrite/rewrite/compare/v8.72.6...v8.72.7)

##### What's Changed

- RPC - Remove mention of JavaScript for Python RPC server error message by [@​greg-at-moderne](https://redirect.github.com/greg-at-moderne) in [#​6673](https://redirect.github.com/openrewrite/rewrite/pull/6673)

- Improving `AddCommentToImport` when it comes to comments that get added at the beginning of the file by [@​steve-aom-elliott](https://redirect.github.com/steve-aom-elliott) in [#​6675](https://redirect.github.com/openrewrite/rewrite/pull/6675)
- Fix DeleteProperty losing newlines when deleting entries with block scalars by [@​Jenson3210](https://redirect.github.com/Jenson3210) in [#​6670](https://redirect.github.com/openrewrite/rewrite/pull/6670)
- Python: Add RPC infrastructure for calling Java recipes by [@​jkschneider](https://redirect.github.com/jkschneider) in [#​6677](https://redirect.github.com/openrewrite/rewrite/pull/6677)
- Python: Add pip bootstrap support for openrewrite package by [@​jkschneider](https://redirect.github.com/jkschneider) in [#​6679](https://redirect.github.com/openrewrite/rewrite/pull/6679)
- Python: Add data table support for recipes by [@​jkschneider](https://redirect.github.com/jkschneider) in [#​6680](https://redirect.github.com/openrewrite/rewrite/pull/6680)
- Add Gradle FindRepositoryOrder recipe by [@​kmccarp](https://redirect.github.com/kmccarp) in [#​6676](https://redirect.github.com/openrewrite/rewrite/pull/6676)
- Python: Add context-insensitive templating mechanism by [@​jkschneider](https://redirect.github.com/jkschneider) in [#​6681](https://redirect.github.com/openrewrite/rewrite/pull/6681)
- Fix test for newer plugin versions by [@​Jenson3210](https://redirect.github.com/Jenson3210) in [#​6685](https://redirect.github.com/openrewrite/rewrite/pull/6685)
- Catch Throwable instead of Exception in KotlinParser and PythonParser by [@​Jenson3210](https://redirect.github.com/Jenson3210) in [#​6633](https://redirect.github.com/openrewrite/rewrite/pull/6633)

**Full Changelog**: <https://github.com/openrewrite/rewrite/compare/v8.72.6...v8.72.7>

[Compare Source](https://redirect.github.com/openrewrite/rewrite/compare/v8.72.5...v8.72.6)

#### What's Changed

- Python: Add MethodMatcher for matching method invocations by type signature by [@​jkschneider](https://redirect.github.com/jkschneider) in [#​6671](https://redirect.github.com/openrewrite/rewrite/pull/6671)
